%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
% The following was adapted from a definition generated by Gemini              %
% https://g.co/gemini/share/bb25cf82c6d7                                       %
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

% Define colors for styling
\definecolor{neslkeywords}{rgb}{0,0,0.6}
\definecolor{neslfunctions}{rgb}{0.1,0.4,0.1}
\definecolor{nesltypes}{rgb}{0.5,0.2,0}
\definecolor{neslconstants}{rgb}{0.5,0,0.5}
\definecolor{neslstrings}{rgb}{0.7,0.1,0.1}
\definecolor{neslcomments}{rgb}{0.4,0.4,0.4}

% Define the NESL language for the listings package
\lstdefinelanguage{NESL}{
  % Treat these characters as keywords
  otherkeywords={|,\{,\},:},
  % Class 1: Primary keywords
  morekeywords={%
    if, then, else, let, in, function, datatype%
  },
  % Class 2: Built-in functions, word operators, and special operators
  keywords=[2]{%
    % Logical
    not, or, and, xor, nor, nand,%
    % Predicates
    plusp, minusp, zerop, oddp, evenp,%
    % Arithmetic
    abs, diff, max, min, rem, lshift, rshift, sqrt, isqrt, ln, log, exp, expt, sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan, sinh, cosh, tanh,%
    % Conversion
    btoi, code_char, char_code, float, ceil, floor, trunc, round,%
    % Other Scalar
    rand,%
    % Sequence Functions
    dist, elt, rep, zip, plus_scan, max_scan, min_scan, or_scan, and_scan, iseq, sum, max_val, min_val, any, all, count, max_index, min_index, permute, rotate, reverse, pack, subseq, drop, take, odd_elts, even_elts, interleave, partition, flatten, split, bottop, head_rest, rest_tail, sort, rank, collect, kth_smallest, search_for_subseqs, remove_duplicates, union, intersection, name,%
    % Misc Functions
    eql, hash, select,%
    % String Manipulation
    linify, wordify, lowercase, uppercase, string_eql, parse_int, parse_float,%
    % I/O and Side Effects
    print_char, print_string, print_debug, write_object_to_file, write_string_to_file, read_object_from_file, read_int_seq_from_file, read_float_seq_from_file, open_in_file, open_out_file, close_file, write_char, write_string, read_char, read_string, read_line, read_word, open_check, write_check, read_check, time,%
    % Plotting & Shell
    make_window, bounding_box, draw_points, draw_lines, draw_segments, draw_strings, get_mouse_info, close_window, shell_command, get_environment_variable, spawn%
  },
  % Class 3: Types and type classes
  keywords=[3]{%
    any, bool, char, float, int, logical, number, ordinal%
  },
  % Class 4: Constants
  keywords=[4]{t, f, pi, space, newline, tab},
  % Misc
  sensitive=false, % NESL is case-insensitive
  morestring=[b]", % Strings are enclosed in double quotes
  morestring=[b]'  % Characters are enclosed in single quotes
}

% Apply styling to the defined classes
\lstset{
  language=NESL,
  keywordstyle=\color{neslkeywords}\bfseries,
  keywordstyle=[2]\color{neslfunctions},
  keywordstyle=[3]\color{nesltypes},
  keywordstyle=[4]\color{neslconstants}\bfseries,
  stringstyle=\color{neslstrings},
  commentstyle=\color{neslcomments},
  basicstyle=\ttfamily\small,
  breaklines=true,
  breakatwhitespace=true,
  tabsize=2,
  numbers=left
}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\begin{document}

\title{Village Synthesis}

\author{Liam Strand}
\affiliation{%
	\institution{Northwestern University}
	\city{Evanston}
	\state{IL}
	\country{USA}
}
\email{ltrs@u.northwestern.edu}
\orcid{0009-0005-9712-1689}

\begin{abstract}
	The cell divides and a hundred million years later, it's
	a bull shark or Sophocles. Not sure which; not sure
	how these things work. Point is:
	things change, evolve, move forward.
	You get the job, get promoted, get fired. Lead
	becomes gold, then one day it doesn't.
	The sun orbits the earth, then one day it doesn't.
	The whale gets legs, gets bored with land, goes back
	to the ocean, the legs are now a metaphor. Nothing certain,
	nothing written in stone that can't be unwritten by the
	hammer. The world keeps trying to end itself because it
	wants to end, then one day it doesn't.
	You get homesick. Get heartburn. Get a strange yearning to
	pray when looking at photographs or cellphone towers. You
	have a hard time adjusting to your new status, station, or
	impending fate. You believed one thing,
	now you believe something else. The earth has ice caps, then
	one day it doesn't. Everything: converted
	to a newer thing. Meanwhile: old gods become lesser gods,
	become priests leaving offerings at someone else's altar.
	The god of war grows up to sell pocketknives at a pawn shop. The
	goddess of carrots peddles sinus medications.
	The messenger god now brings your mail--
	and there he is, right on schedule, one house to the next.
	This always stuns me: the way an envelope arrives; how we still
	reach toward one another, how this correspondence endures:
	one figure approaches your door with a satchel full of sand,
	pigeon feathers, sorrows, and names.
\end{abstract}

\keywords{Nested-Data Parallelism, Heterogeneity, FPGA, HDL Synthesis}

		NOT_B -> B_TO_I [headlabel="0 "];
		ADD_I -> NOT_I [headlabel="0 "];
		NOT_I -> MUL_I [headlabel="0 "];
		B_TO_I -> MUL_I [headlabel="1 "];
		MUL_I -> I_TO_F [headlabel="0 "];
		I_TO_F -> DIV_F [headlabel="0 "];
		"IN_F(3)" -> DIV_F [headlabel="1 "];
		DIV_F -> F_TO_I [headlabel="0 "]
		F_TO_I -> "OUT_I(0)" [headlabel="0 "];
		}
		\label{fig:teaser}
		\caption{The abstract representation of a VIR operator dataflow subgraph that can now be lowered to hardware.}
	\end{center}
\end{teaserfigure}

\section{Introduction}

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

\section{Background}

\subsection{Village}

Implementing complex algorithms in low-level parallel languages like CUDA or OpenMP is challenging.
A direct implementation may be relatively easy, but taking full advantage of hardware capabilities often requires complex, inscrutable code that obfuscates the underlying algorithm.
High-level parallel languages, like Chapel \cite{chamberlain2015chapel}, Bend \cite{bend-hvm2}, or NESL \cite{BLELLOCH-NESL-JPDC94, BLELLOCH-NSEL-ICFP96} can ergonomically express parallel algorithms, but their implementations can struggle to be as performant as their low-level counterparts because the programmer does not have direct access to hardware resources.

The Village project provides a new implementation of NESL that aims to preserve NESL's expressive high-level parallel semantics and generate code that has the performance of a low-level parallel language.
It does this by lowering NESL to VCODE, then VCODE to VIR, and finally VIR to LLVM-IR, which is handed off to the LLVM backend to generate a static executable.
In this work, we intercept the compilation process at the VIR stage, and instead lower the program to a hardware design.

\subsubsection{NESL}

Designed in 1992 and implemented in 1996, NESL is a high-level parallel language that uses the nested data parallelism model of computation.
This model allows programmers to express the parallelism of their algorithms in terms of applying a function in parallel to a set of values, where those values could themselves be parallel functions.
It is very flexible and allows the programmer to express all of the parallelism available in their algorithm.

\begin{figure}
	\begin{center}
		\begin{lstlisting}[language=NESL]
function qsort(a) =
  if (#a < 2) then a
  else
    let pivot  = a[#a/2];
        lesser = {e in a | e < pivot};
        equal  = {e in a | e == pivot};
        greater= {e in a | e > pivot};
        result = {qsort(v): v in [lesser,greater]}
    in result[0] ++ equal ++ result[1];
		\end{lstlisting}
	\end{center}
	\caption{A NESL implementation of quicksort, exposing the parallelism of the scan over \texttt{a} and the parallel recursive calls to \texttt{qsort}.}\label{fig:nesl-sample}
\end{figure}

Figure~\ref{fig:nesl-sample} contains the NESL implementation of quicksort, a classic parallel algorithm.
Lines 5-7 express flat data parallelism, with each line applying a filter to the collection.
Line 8 shows the recursive calls over the two subcollections, which can be executed in parallel.
A flat data parallel language would struggle to reveal the parallelism available in quicksort so succinctly and clearly.
